ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general omurice entry; no specific pork katsu curry omurice variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with meat and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific pork katsu curry omurice code. A0FJP (rice-based dish with meat and sauce) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific composite Japanese dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork katsu curry omurice as served in a Japanese restaurant or school canteen typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising fried rice wrapped in egg omelette topped with a pork katsu cutlet and curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Katsu Curry Omurice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Katsu Curry Omurice (cr_id F05100106). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (deep-fried breaded pork cutlet with egg fried rice) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; White rice GI reference (Jasmine/short-grain cooked white rice, GI ~72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork katsu curry omurice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database). The breaded pork katsu and curry sauce contribute fat and protein that may modestly lower the effective GI, but the white rice base dominates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 72 × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.1 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.5, rounded to 32.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 357 mg/100 g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: At 357 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,250 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — and the low potassium content (estimated below 200 mg/100 g) provides insufficient counterbalance; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium curry sauce, and avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g) driving rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 72 and the dish provides only 0.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 32.6 per standard serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice, reduce portion size, and avoid extra curry sauce which may contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated fat from deep-frying (11.47 g total fat/100 g) including saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying the pork katsu generates saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) that raise LDL cholesterol and promote arterial inflammation, while the sodium load of 357 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose baked or air-fried katsu alternatives and limit serving frequency to occasional consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat that amplify joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried pork katsu generates AGEs and contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) from pork that amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request baked or air-fried katsu and confirm that curry sauce does not contain nightshade ingredients such as tomato or chili.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Katsu Curry Omurice (ポークカツカレーオムライス) is a Japanese fusion dish combining three elements: omurice (fried rice wrapped in a thin egg omelette), a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu-style), and Japanese curry sauce. The fried rice base is typically seasoned with soy sauce, ketchup, or demi-glace and stir-fried with onion and vegetables before being enveloped in a soft, lightly cooked egg omelette. The pork loin or shoulder is coated in flour, egg wash, and panko breadcrumbs, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. Japanese curry sauce — thickened with roux and flavoured with onion, carrot, potato, and curry spice blend — is ladled over the assembled dish. The result is a rich, savoury, mildly spiced plate with contrasting textures: crispy katsu, silky omelette, and tender rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 188 kcal, 8.19 g protein, 11.47 g fat, and 12.89 g carbohydrate, with 357 mg sodium. The high fat content reflects deep-frying; the moderate protein comes from pork and egg. Dietary fibre is low at 0.7 g/100 g. Clinically, the dish is high concern for type 2 diabetes (high-GI white rice, low fibre, GL ~32.6 per serving), hypertension (357 mg sodium/100 g, ~1,250 mg per plate), and cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat from deep-frying). It is commonly served in Japanese restaurants, school canteens, and fast-casual dining in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100106, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a deep-fried breaded pork and egg fried rice composite dish.
notes: Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for deep-fried breaded pork cutlet with egg fried rice; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) carried forward from input health_suitability annotation; not directly reported by HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for pork katsu curry omurice exists in approved sources. Cultural tags in input listed primary_cuisine as 'mamak' — this appears to be a data entry error; the dish is Japanese in origin and has been reclassified accordingly. All core macros (protein_g 8.19, fat_g 11.47, carbohydrate_g 12.89) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
